questions.question: Explain the difference between function templates and class templates in C++. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Function templates define a family of functions for different types
Class templates define a family of classes for different types
Function templates: used for generic algorithms like findMax, swap, sorting
Class templates: used for generic containers like Stack, Queue, Array
Class templates require angle brackets with type when creating objects

questions.question: What is template specialization? Differentiate between full and partial specialization with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Template specialization provides custom implementations for specific types
Full specialization: all template parameters are replaced with concrete types (template<> syntax)
Partial specialization: only some parameters specialized, others remain generic
Partial specialization is only available for class templates, not function templates
Example: template<> for char*, or template<typename T> for Array<T*, 10>

questions.question: What are non-type template parameters? Give an example where they are useful.
keyPoints: Non-type template parameters accept constant values as arguments
They can be integral types, pointers, or references
Useful for specifying sizes, capacities, or fixed values at compile-time
Example: template<int size> class FixedArray creates arrays of fixed size
The value must be a compile-time constant

questions.question: Explain the concept of template instantiation in C++. Differentiate between implicit and explicit instantiation.
keyPoints: Template instantiation creates concrete code from the template blueprint
Implicit instantiation: compiler automatically generates code when template is used
Explicit instantiation: programmer forces generation using 'template' keyword with specific types
Syntax for explicit: template class Stack<int>; or template void swap<int>(int&, int&);
Explicit instantiation is useful to reduce compilation overhead in large projects

questions.question: What is SFINAE? How is it useful in template metaprogramming?
keyPoints: SFINAE stands for Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
It occurs during template argument deduction
Invalid substitutions are discarded rather than causing errors
Used to enable/disable template functions based on type capabilities
Enables compile-time type checking and function selection without errors

questions.question: Write a C++ program to implement a generic Stack class template with push, pop, and display operations. Include exception handling for stack overflow and underflow conditions.
keyPoints: Define class template with type parameter template<typename T>
Include private members: array/vector of type T, top index, capacity
Implement push(T): check overflow, increment top, store element
Implement pop(): check underflow, return and decrement top
Implement display(): traverse and print elements from bottom to top
Use exceptions or error messages for boundary conditions
Main function demonstrates with int and string stacks

questions.question: Explain templates with default arguments. Write a class template for an Array that accepts a default type of int and a default size of 10. Also demonstrate partial specialization for pointer types.
keyPoints: Templates can have default arguments like functions
Example: template<typename T = int, int size = 10> class Array
Default arguments used when user doesn't specify them
Partial specialization for pointers: template<typename T> class Array<T*, 10>
Show complete code with default template parameters
Demonstrate specialized version behavior with pointer types
Show how different instantiations work

questions.question: Write a function template to find the maximum element in an array. Extend it to handle both primitive types and user-defined types by using template specialization.
keyPoints: Function template: template<typename T> T findMax(T arr[], int n)
Use comparison operator (>) for finding maximum
For primitive types: direct comparison works
For user-defined types: require operator overloading
Full specialization for char* to compare strings using strcmp
Or use template with constraint requiring comparison capability
Main demonstrates with int, float, and string arrays

questions.question: What are the advantages and limitations of using templates in C++?
keyPoints: Advantages: Code reusability, type safety, no runtime overhead, static polymorphism
Limitations: Increased compilation time, code bloat (multiple instantiations), complex error messages
Templates enable STL and generic programming
Debugging can be difficult due to cryptic error messages
Templates are resolved at compile-time, not runtime
